summary: South Korea's National Assembly passed a law prohibiting app store operators from forcing developers to use specific payment methods, effectively breaking Apple and Google's 30% commission stranglehold. DHH argues this is a watershed moment not because of South Korea's market size, but because it cracks the dam of arguments Apple and Google have used to defend their payment mandates worldwide. He predicts app store cuts will drop to 5-10%, and that competing payment processors like Stripe will drive innovation. DHH frames this as personal vindication after testifying before multiple U.S. legislative bodies and seeing those efforts defeated by Apple and Google's lobbying.
key_points: South Korea passed a law prohibiting forced app store payment methods, the first real legislative defeat for Apple and Google's 30% cut
The significance is less about South Korea's market and more about setting precedent that other countries can follow
App store payment processing requires no innovation to operate, just dominant market power, making competition inevitable once mandates fall
DHH connects this to his personal fight after Apple nearly killed HEY.com and his multiple legislative testimonies in the U.S.
Payment innovation from companies like Stripe will likely outcompete Apple and Google's own solutions once the market opens
money_quotes: Apple and Google knew from the start of this fight that they couldn't afford to lose even once.
Now they've finally met a legislature they couldn't bully or buy: The National Assembly of the Republic of Korea.
It's the crack in the dam. The one that'll soon flood their scarecrow arguments on app-store payment mandates around the world.
They require little to no innovation to operate, just dominant market power, which both companies have in excess.
Reality is going to refute the fear that Apple and Google have been working so hard to stoke.
It always seems impossible until it's done.
Apple and Google set fire to their relationship with developers to forestall the inevitable.
key_insight: South Korea's app store payment law matters not for its local impact but as the first crack in a dam that will force Apple and Google to compete on merit rather than market power for developer payment processing.
